title: if/elif/else and Python truthiness

glossary.definition: How Python decides if a value is considered true or false in an if-statement without you having to explicitly check.
Values that Python automatically treats as false, such as zero, empty lists, or None.

overview.why: Truthiness lets you write concise guards (if items: instead of if len(items) > 0:). The danger is when 0 or '' are valid values that must NOT be treated as 'missing' — a naked truthiness check would silently skip them.
where_used: FastAPI route guards (if not current_user: raise 401)

why_learning_this: Writing correct guards in FastAPI handlers that distinguish None from 0 or an empty string
Understanding why if value: can silently hide a valid 0 — the foundation of safe API input validation

common_mistakes.title: Using truthiness when 0 or '' are valid inputs
common_mistakes.explanation: if count: returns False for count=0, silently skipping valid data. Fix: use explicit checks (if count is None:) when 0 is a meaningful value.

common_mistakes.title: Forgetting elif stops the chain
common_mistakes.explanation: Using separate if statements instead of elif means multiple branches can execute. Use elif when conditions are mutually exclusive.

recall_questions.q: Which values does Python treat as falsy?
recall_questions.answer: False, None, 0, 0.0, empty string, empty list, empty dict, and empty set.

recall_questions.q: If the first branch of an if/elif chain matches, what happens to the remaining elif and else branches?
recall_questions.answer: They are all skipped — only the first matching branch executes.
When 0 or an empty string are valid values that should not be treated as missing — a truthiness check would silently skip them.

recall_questions.q: What is the difference between chaining elif vs using separate if statements?
recall_questions.answer: elif is part of the same chain — only the first match runs. Separate if statements are independent — multiple can run if multiple conditions are true.

practice_tasks.title: HTTP status classifier
practice_tasks.prompt: Modify `classify_status(code)` to return 'success' for 2xx codes, 'client_error' for 4xx, 'server_error' for 5xx, and 'unknown' for anything else. Use if/elif/else.

code_walkthrough.focus: items (empty list) and count (zero) are falsy so their blocks are skipped; name ('alice') is truthy so only 'has name' prints before 'done'.

challenge.title: Config timeout guard
challenge.prompt: Write `get_timeout(config, default)` that returns `config['timeout']` when it is present, not None, and a positive integer. Return `default` in all other cases. Use explicit checks, not truthiness.

understanding_checks.question: What will this code print?
understanding_checks.why: Python evaluates an if/elif chain top-down and executes only the FIRST matching block. Since `404 >= 400` is true, it prints 'error' and skips the remaining branches, even though `status == 404` is also true.

understanding_checks.question: This code is intended to use the `timeout` from the config, but falls back to 10 if missing. It fails when `timeout=0` is passed in (which is a valid setting). Why?
understanding_checks.answer: It evaluates truthiness, and `0` is falsy, so it incorrectly falls back to `10`.
understanding_checks.why: Truthiness checks like `not timeout` treat `0` as falsy. When 0 is a valid meaningful value, you must use an explicit check like `if timeout is None:`.
